title: Mediterranean
subtitle: Greek and Roman antiquity — the classical forms that Europe keeps returning to.
intro: The classical world's obsession with the human body, proportion, and idealized form shaped every European revival that came after — Renaissance, Neoclassical, and on. Much of it survives only in fragments.

works.title: Palace of Knossos
works.desc: A sprawling Bronze Age palace with running water, frescoes of dolphins and bull-leapers, and a storage maze that may be the origin of the Minotaur myth.

works.title: The Parthenon
works.desc: Temple to Athena on the Acropolis. The columns lean inward and swell slightly in the middle — optical corrections so they look perfectly straight.

works.title: Charioteer of Delphi
works.desc: One of the few surviving Greek bronzes. His calm expression, inset with glass and copper eyes, is a moment in a victory lap — all force held still.

works.title: Discobolus
works.desc: Myron's discus thrower — the original bronze is lost; only Roman marble copies survive. A moment just before release, coiled and still.

works.title: Venus de Milo
works.desc: Marble Aphrodite found on the island of Milos in 1820. The missing arms are part of her fame — we don't know what she was doing.

works.title: Winged Victory of Samothrace
works.desc: Nike landing on the prow of a ship, wind pressing her drapery against her body. One of the most physically dynamic pieces of ancient sculpture.

works.title: Laocoön and His Sons
works.desc: A Trojan priest and his sons strangled by sea serpents. Dug up in a vineyard in 1506 and hauled to the Vatican. Michelangelo studied it for weeks.

works.title: Augustus of Prima Porta
works.desc: The emperor posed as a general, barefoot like a god. The breastplate shows the return of captured Roman standards — a diplomatic win dressed as conquest.

works.title: The Colosseum
works.desc: An elliptical amphitheater for 50,000 spectators. The underground hypogeum ran elevators, flooded the floor for sea battles, and housed wild animals.

works.title: Trajan's Column
works.desc: A 100-foot marble column whose spiral frieze narrates the Dacian Wars in 155 continuous scenes — history carved as a comic strip.

works.title: Pompeii
works.desc: Vesuvius sealed a whole Roman town in volcanic ash. Frescoes, bread loaves, graffiti, and the shapes of bodies all survived — daily life frozen.

works.title: Riace Bronzes
works.desc: Two life-size bronze warriors pulled from the sea off Calabria in 1972. Their teeth are silver, their eyelashes hammered copper.

works.title: Aphrodite of Knidos
works.desc: The first free-standing nude female figure in Greek sculpture — scandalous enough that men climbed her sanctuary walls at night. Only copies survive.

works.title: Kroisos Kouros
works.desc: A young man stepping forward, hands at his sides, slight smile — the archaic formula. Found on a grave at Anavyssos, one leg at a time.

works.title: Ara Pacis
works.desc: The Altar of Peace. The imperial family processes in carved marble, framed by acanthus tendrils. Augustus inserts himself into the mythic past.

works.title: Pantheon
works.desc: The largest unreinforced concrete dome ever built. The oculus at its center has been open to the sky for nineteen centuries — rain falls through it into drains in the floor.

works.title: Apoxyomenos
works.desc: An athlete scraping oil from his arm. The Roman marble copy stands in for a lost bronze original by Alexander the Great's favorite sculptor.

works.title: Sarcophagus of the Spouses
works.desc: A painted terracotta couple reclining together on their tomb lid — smiling, touching, treated as equals. Unthinkable in contemporary Greek or Egyptian funerary art.

works.title: Doryphoros
works.desc: The "Spear Bearer" — a treatise on ideal human proportion made in marble. Polykleitos wrote a book called the Canon about it; only this statue survives.

works.title: Dying Gaul
works.desc: A defeated Celt collapsing on his shield, wound dripping. Pergamene art showed the enemy as human — a compliment paid in marble, after the kill.

works.title: Boxer at Rest
works.desc: A bronze boxer after a fight — cauliflower ears, bloodied lip, exhausted. One of the few surviving Greek originals, not a later copy.

works.title: Boy with Thorn
works.desc: A boy pulling a thorn from his foot, head bent in total concentration. One of the first ancient bronzes to be widely copied in the Renaissance.

works.title: Hadrian's Villa
works.desc: A 250-acre retreat where the emperor built scale models of the sights from his tour of the empire — a Nile canal, an Athenian stoa, a Canopus.
